front: What is the Ad hoc model?
back:
“Build and fix” model (also referred to as an “ad hoc” model)

  • Software is developed without any specification or design
  • Initial product is built, which is then repeatedly modified until it (software) satisfies the user
  • User checks whether the desired functions 'are present. If not, then the software is changed according to the needs by adding, modifying or deleting functions.
This process goes on until the user feels that the software can be used productively.

However, the lack of design requirements and repeated modifications result in loss of acceptability of software. Thus, software engineers are strongly discouraged from using this development approach.

front: What are the Advantages of the Ad hoc model?
back:
• Requires less experience to execute or manage other than the ability to program.
  • Suitable for smaller software.
  • Requires less project planning.

front: What are the Disadvantages of the Ad hoc model?
back:
• No real means is available of assessing the progress, quality, and risks.
  • Cost of using this process model is high as it requires rework until user's requirements are accomplished.
  • Informal design of the software as it involves unplanned procedure.
  • Maintenance of these models is problematic.

front: What are Software Development Models?
back:
• Tools for planning, performing, controlling software development projects
  • Various phases, executed one after the other in a fixed sequence, building on each other
  • Defined milestones in between

Due to complexity of IT projects various development models have been established:
  • To minimize development risks
  • To make development status transparent

front: What is the definition of Software Development Models?
back:
A development/procedure model represents a development plan for conception, production, and maintenance of software products or systems which defines the procedure of software development by using defined phases, activties, and tools.

Selecting the appropriate development model is crucial for IT projects‘ success and quality and for meeting time and cost restrictions (magic triangle).

front: What is the Magic Triangle of IT/Software Development Projects?
back:
The magic triangle describes the three competing constraints that must be balanced in every project:
  • Scope & Quality → to be maximized
  • Time → to be minimized
  • Cost → to be minimized
Changing one constraint affects the others. Selecting the appropriate development model is crucial for meeting these time and cost restrictions and for project success and quality.

front: What is the basic structure identical and what are common phases?
back:
From a preliminary conception to an executable software

Common phases:
  1. Problem analysis: analyzing the business case
  2. Conception: draft of future business processes or applications
  3. System design: Transfer of optimized business processes and data structures in a codable model
  4. Realization: Programming/coding
  5. Test: Check functionality on the base of test cases
  6. Rollout: Deployment and startup in „production“ – operation
  7. Maintenance: Bugfixing, support and new releases

front: Which different categories of Software Development Models do exist?
back:
Classical models (Sequential procedure models):
  • Waterfall model
  • Spirtal model
Modern models:
  • V-model
  • RationalUnified Process (RUP) model
Agile models:
  • Extreme programming
  • Scrum

front: How does the Waterfall model look like?
back:
A linear-sequential model: each phase produces an output that becomes the input of the next phase (no overlapping):
Feasibility Study → Requirements Analysis (→ SRS) → Design (→ Software Architecture) → Implementation/Coding (→ Software) → Verification/Testing (→ Test & Debug) → Deployment (→ Release) → Maintenance (→ New Release).

front: What are the characteristics of the waterfall model?
back:
The Waterfall Model was the first Process Model to be introduced.

It is also referred to as a linearsequential life cycle model, which is not adaptive.

It is very simple to understand and use. The Waterfall Model is organized in subsequently following phases; each phase must be completed before the next phase can begin and there is no overlapping in the phases. The outcome of one phase acts as the input for the next phase sequentially.

front: What are the different Phases of the waterfall model (more details)?
back:
• Requirement Gathering and analysis − All possible requirements of the system to be developed are captured in this phase and documented in a requirement specification document.

  • System Design − The requirement specifications from first phase are studied in this phase and the system design is prepared. This system design helps in specifying hardware and system requirements and helps in defining the overall system architecture.

  • Implementation − With inputs from the system design, the system is first developed in small programs called units, which are integrated in the next phase. Each unit is developed and tested for its functionality, which is referred to as Unit Testing.

  • Integration and Testing − All the units developed in the implementation phase are integrated into a system after testing of each unit. Post integration the entire system is tested for any faults and failures.

  • Deployment of system − Once the functional and non-functional testing is done, the product is deployed in the customer environment or released into the market.

  • Maintenance − There are some issues which come up in the client environment. To fix those issues, patches are released. Also to enhance the product some better versions are released. Maintenance is done to deliver these changes in the customer environment

front: What are applications of the waterfall model?
back:
• Requirements are very well documented, clear and fixed.
  • Product definition is stable.
  • Technology is understood and is not dynamic.
  • There are no ambiguous requirements.
  • Ample resources with required expertise are available to support the product.
  • The project is short.

front: How does the spiral model look like?
What are the characteristics of the spiral model?
back: The spiral model combines the idea of iterative development with the systematic, controlled aspects of the waterfall model. This Spiral model is a combination of iterative development process model and sequential linear development model i.e. the waterfall model with a very high emphasis on risk analysis. It allows incremental releases of the product or incremental refinement through each iteration around the spiral.

front: What are the 4 phases of the spiral model?
back:
The spiral model has four phases. A software project repeatedly passes through these phases in iterations called Spirals.
  • Planning/SRS (Identification): This phase starts with gathering the business requirements in the baseline spiral. In the subsequent spirals as the product matures, identification of system requirements, subsystem requirements and unit requirements are all done in this phase. This phase also includes understanding the system requirements by continuous communication between the customer and the system analyst.

  • Design & Risk Analysis: The Design phase starts with the conceptual design in the baseline spiral and involves architectural design, logical design of modules, physical product design and the final design in the subsequent spirals. Risk Analysis includes identifying, estimating and monitoring the technical feasibility and management risks, such as schedule slippage and cost overrun

  • Coding (Construct or Build): The Construct phase refers to production of the actual software product at every spiral. In the baseline spiral, when the product is just thought of and the design is being developed a POC (Proof of Concept) is developed in this phase to get customer feedback. Then in the subsequent spirals with higher clarity on requirements and design details a working model of the software called build is produced with a version number. These builds are sent to the customer for feedback.

  • Testing (Evaluation): After testing the build, at the end of first iteration, the customer evaluates the software and provides feedback.

front: What are the application of the spiral model?
back:
The Spiral Model is widely used in the software industry as it is in sync with the natural development process of any product, i.e. learning with maturity which involves minimum risk for the customer as well as the development firms.
  • When there is a budget constraint and risk evaluation is important.
  • For medium to high-risk projects.
  • Long-term project commitment because of potential changes to economic priorities as the requirements change with time.
  • Customer is not sure of their requirements which is usually the case.
  • Requirements are complex and need evaluation to get clarity.
  • New product line which should be released in phases to get enough customer feedback.
  • Significant changes are expected in the product during the development cycle.

front: How does the V-Model look like?
back:
The V-Model pairs each development/specification phase (left, going down) with a corresponding test phase (right, going up); the coding phase sits at the bottom and joins the two sides:
  • Concept of Operations ↔ Acceptance Test
  • System Requirements ↔ System Test
  • Subsystem Requirements ↔ Subsystem Test
  • Subsystem (Architecture) Design ↔ Integration Test
  • Unit Design ↔ Unit Test
Verification phases are on the left side, validation phases on the right.

front: What are the characteristics of the V-Model?
back:
The V-Model is an extension of the waterfall model and is based on the association of a testing phase for each corresponding development stage. This means that for every single phase in the development cycle, there is a directly associated testing phase. This is a highly-disciplined model and the next phase starts only after completion of the previous phase.

Under the V-Model, the corresponding testing phase of the development phase is planned in parallel. So, there are Verification phases on one side of the ‘V’ and Validation phases on the other side. The Coding Phase joins the two sides of the V-Model.

front: What are the phases of the V-Model?
back:
Business Requirement Analysis – Concept of Operations: This is the first phase in the development cycle where the product requirements are understood from the customer’s perspective. This phase involves detailed communication with the customer to understand his expectations and exact requirement. This is a very important activity and needs to be managed well, as most of the customers are not sure about what exactly they need. The acceptance test design planning is done at this stage as business requirements can be used as an input for acceptance testing.

System Design: Once you have the clear and detailed product requirements, it is time to design the complete system. The system design will have the understanding and detailing the complete hardware and communication setup for the product under development. The system test plan is developed based on the system design. Doing this at an earlier stage leaves more time for the actual test execution later.

Architectural Design (Subsystem Design): Architectural specifications are understood and designed in this phase. Usually more than one technical approach is proposed and based on the technical and financial feasibility the final decision is taken. The system design is broken down further into modules taking up different functionality. This is also referred to as High Level Design (HLD).
The data transfer and communication between the internal modules and with the outside world (other systems) is clearly understood and defined in this stage. With this information, integration tests can be designed and documented during this stage.

Module Design (Unit Design): In this phase, the detailed internal design for all the system modules is specified, referred to as Low Level Design (LLD). It is important that the design is compatible with the other modules in the system architecture and the other external systems. The unit tests are an essential part of any development process and helps eliminate the maximum faults and errors at a very early stage. These unit tests can be designed at this stage based on the internal module designs.

Coding Phase: The actual coding of the system modules designed in the design phase is taken up in the Coding phase. The best suitable programming language is decided based on the system and architectural requirements. The coding is performed based on the coding guidelines and standards. The code goes through numerous code reviews and is optimized for best performance before the final build is checked into the repository.

Unit Testing: Unit tests designed in the module design phase are executed on the code during this validation phase. Unit testing is the testing at code level and helps eliminate bugs at an early stage, though all defects cannot be uncovered by unit testing

Integration Testing: Integration testing is associated with the architectural design phase. Integration tests are performed to test the coexistence and communication of the internal modules within the system.

System Testing: System testing is directly associated with the system design phase. System tests check the entire system functionality and the communication of the system under development with external systems. Most of the software and hardware compatibility issues can be uncovered during this system test execution.

Acceptance Testing: Acceptance testing is associated with the business requirement analysis phase and involves testing the product in user environment. Acceptance tests uncover the compatibility issues with the other systems available in the user environment. It also discovers the non-functional issues such as load and performance defects in the actual user environment

front: What are applications of the V-Model?
back:
V- Model application is almost the same as the waterfall model, as both the models are of sequential type. Requirements have to be very clear before the project starts, because it is usually expensive to go back and make changes. This model is used in the medical development field, as it is strictly a disciplined domain.
  • Requirements well defined, clearly documented and fixed.
  • Product definition is stable.
  • Technology is not dynamic and is well understood by the project team.
  • There are no ambiguous or undefined requirements.
  • The project is short.

front: What are the advantages of the V-Model?
back:
The advantage of the V-Model method is that it is very easy to understand and apply. The simplicity of this model also makes it easier to manage. The disadvantage is that the model is not flexible to changes and just in case there is a requirement change, which is very common in today’s dynamic world, it becomes very expensive to make the change.

Advantages of the V-Model method:
  • Phases are completed one at a time
  • Works well for smaller projects where requirements are very well understood
  • Simple and easy to understand and use
  • Easy to manage

front: What are the disadvantages of the V-Model?
back:
The disadvantages of the V-Model method are as follows:
  • High risk and uncertainty.
  • Not a good model for complex and object-oriented projects.
  • Poor model for long and ongoing projects.
  • Not suitable for the projects where requirements are at a moderate to high risk of changing.
  • Once an application is in the testing stage, it is difficult to go back and change a functionality.
  • No working software is produced until late during the life cycle.

front: How does the V-Model XT (Extreme Tailoring) look like?
What is V-Model XT (Extreme Tailoring)?
back: The V – Model XT is a (German) model that works well for development of a project which has the constraints about time, budget and the most important features: the project has to have the specified functionality. This model has a complex structure of components, including Project Types, Process Modules, Project Execution Strategies. These components are connected with others and with the ‘extended tailoring’ provide a new methodically way to develop system and project.

front: What is the Rational Unified Process Model?
back:
Organization along content: Disciplines
Timeline: Phases & Iterations
Milestones: At the end of each phase: critical decisions must be made, key goals must have been achieved

front: What are the Main Characteristics of Rational Unified Process Model?
back: The Rational Unified Process (RUP) Model is based on sound software engineering principles such as taking an iterative, requirements-driven, and architecture-centric approach to software development.

front: What are the different disciplines of the Rational Unified Process Model?
back:
Development Disciplines:
Business Modeling: The goal is to understand the business of the organization, usually confined to the scope of the business that is relevant to the system being developed.

Requirements: The goal is to elicit, document, and agree upon the scope of what is and what is not to be built. This information is used by analysts, designers, and programmers to build the system, by testers to verify the system, and by the project manager to plan and manage the project.

Analysis & Design: The goal is to analyze the requirements for the system and to design a solution to be implemented, taking into consideration the requirements, constraints and all applicable standards and guidelines.

Implementation: The goal is to transform the design into executable code and to perform a basic level of testing, in particular unit testing.

Test: The goal is to perform an objective evaluation to ensure quality. This includes finding defects, validating that system works as designed, and verifying that the requirements are met.

Deployment: The goal is to plan for the delivery of the system and to execute the plan to make the system available to end users.

Support Disciplines: 
Configuration and Change Management: The goal is to manage access to the project’s work products. This includes not only tracking versions over time but also controlling and managing changes to them.

Project Management: The goal is to direct the activities that take place on the project. This includes managing risks, directing people (assigning tasks, tracking progress, etc.), and coordinating with people and systems outside the scope of the project to be sure that it is delivered on time and within budget.

Environment: The goal is to support the rest of the effort in terms in ensuring that the proper process, guidance (standards and guidelines), and tools (hardware, software, etc.) are available for the team as needed

front: What are the 4 Phases and their Milestones in the Rational Unified Process (RUP)?
back:
RUP organizes its timeline into 4 phases, each ending with a milestone where critical go/no-go decisions must be made:
  • Inception – define project scope, estimate cost & schedule, define risks, develop business case → Milestone: Lifecycle Objectives (LCO)
  • Elaboration – specify requirements in detail, validate & evolve architecture, staff the team → Milestone: Lifecycle Architecture (LCA)
  • Construction – model, build and test the system, develop supporting documentation → Milestone: Initial Operational Capability (IOC)
  • Transition – system testing, user testing, system rework & deployment → Milestone: Product Release (PR)

front: What are the Advantages of the Rational Unified Process Model?
back:
• Allows adaptive capability to deal with changing requirements throughout the development life cycle
  • Emphasizes the need of accurate documentation.
  • Forcing integration to occur throughout development.

front: What are the Disadvantages of the Rational Unified Process Model?
back:
• Heavily relies on proficient and expert team members
  • Given the emphasis on integration throughout the development process
  • RUP is a fairly complicated model.

front: What are the 4 core values of the Manifesto for Agile Software Development?
back:
We value the items on the left more than those on the right:
  • Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
  • Working software over comprehensive documentation
  • Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
  • Responding to change over following a plan
That is, while there is value in the items on the right, we value the items on the left more.

front: What are the principles of the Manifesto for Agile Software Development?
back:
• Our highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software.
  • Welcome changing requirements, even late in development. Agile processes harness change for the customer's competitive advantage.
  • Deliver working software frequently, from a couple of weeks to a couple of months, with a preference to the shorter timescale.
  • Business people and developers must work together daily throughout the project.
  • Build projects around motivated individuals. Give them the environment and support they need, and trust them to get the job done.
  • The most efficient and effective method of conveying information to and within a development team is face-to-face conversation.
  • Working software is the primary measure of progress.
  • Agile processes promote sustainable development. The sponsors, developers, and users should be able to maintain a constant pace indefinitely.
  • Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design enhances agility.
  • Simplicity--the art of maximizing the amount of work not done--is essential.
  • The best architectures, requirements, and designs emerge from self-organizing teams.
  • At regular intervals, the team reflects on how to become more effective, then tunes and adjusts its behavior accordingl

front: What is Extreme Programming (XP) and main characteristics?
back:
• Extreme Programming improves a software project in five essential ways; communication, simplicity, feedback, respect, and courage.
  • Extreme Programmers constantly communicate with their customers and fellow programmers.
  • They keep their design simple and clean.
  • They get feedback by testing their software starting on day one.
  • They deliver the system to the customers as early as possible and implement changes as suggested. Every small success deepens their respect for the unique contributions of each and every team member.
  • With this foundation Extreme Programmers are able to courageously respond to changing requirements and technology

front: What are the different rules/phases of Extreme Programming?
back:
Planning:
  • User stories are written
  • Release planning creates the release schedule.
  • Make frequent small releases.
  • The project is divided into iterations.
  • Iteration planning starts each iteration.
  • A stand-up meeting starts each day.
Designing:
  • Simplicity. Always do the simplest thing that could possibly work next.
  • Choose a system metaphor.
  • Use CRC cards for design sessions.
  • Create spike solutions to reduce risk.
  • No functionality is added early.
  • Refactor whenever and wherever possible.
Coding:
  • The customer is always available.
  • Code must be written to agreed standards.
  • Code the unit test first.
  • All production code is pair programmed.
  • Only one pair integrates code at a time.
  • Integrate often.
  • Set up a dedicated integration computer.
  • Use collective ownership.
Testing:
  • All code must have unit tests.
  • All code must pass all unit tests before it can be released.
  • When a bug is found tests are created.
  • Acceptance tests are run often and the score is published.

front: What are the advantages of extreme programming?
back:
• Emphasis customer involvement
  • helps establish rational plans and schedules and to get the developers personally committed to their schedules
  • This model is consistent with most modern development methods

front: What are the disadvantages of extreme programming?
back:
• Only as effective as the people involved; Agile does not solve this issue.
  • Requires meetings at frequent intervals.
  • Requires too much development changes 
  • Tends to be impossible to be known exact estimates of work effort at the start of project

front: What is the difference between a User Story and a Use Case?
back:
User stories:
A user story provides a short descriptive sentence that outlines the who, what and why of one or a set of software requirements. User stories put context around interactions, which enables developers to focus their efforts on perspectives, features, functionality and results.

Here's a generic format for a user story:
“A (user/item) should be able to (do something) so that an (outcome) can be achieved” “As a (role) I want (something) so that (benefit)”

Use cases:
A use case expresses a functional software requirement by describing a behavior or interaction, often in the form of a flow or a dialog. The focus of a use case is on cause and effect, as it usually outlines how a user or item interacts with software, as well as how that system responds to those actions.

Here's a general use case format:
“A user does (A), the system responds (B)”

Use cases generally provide more detail and a deeper understanding of functional behaviors to contextualize a software requirement.

front: (J2) What are Software Development Models and the common phases?
back:
Models are tools for planning, performing and controlling software-development projects (fixed phase sequence, milestones). Basic structure is identical — from a preliminary conception to executable software.
Common phases: Problem analysis → Conception → System design → Realization (coding) → Test → Rollout → Maintenance.
Categories: classical sequential (Waterfall, Spiral), modern (V-Model, RUP), agile (XP, Scrum).

front: (J2) Waterfall Model
back: Linear-sequential model — each phase output feeds the next, no overlap: Feasibility → Requirements → Design → Implementation → Verification/Testing → Deployment → Maintenance.

front: (J2) Spiral Model
back: Combines iterative development with the systematic, controlled aspects of the waterfall, with high emphasis on risk analysis. Four phases per spiral: Planning/SRS → Design & Risk Analysis → Coding/Build → Testing/Evaluation.

front: (J2) V-Model
back: Extension of the waterfall: every development/specification phase (left, down) has a corresponding test phase (right, up); coding joins the two sides. Verification on the left, validation on the right.

front: (J2) Rational Unified Process (RUP)
back: Iterative, requirements-driven, architecture-centric process. Organized along disciplines (content) and a timeline of phases & iterations, with milestones (go/no-go) at the end of each phase.

front: (J2) Agile Manifesto – core idea
back: We value: Individuals & interactions over processes/tools; working software over documentation; customer collaboration over contract negotiation; responding to change over following a plan. Principles: early & continuous delivery, welcome change, frequent delivery, daily collaboration, motivated individuals, face-to-face communication, working software as measure of progress, sustainable pace, technical excellence, simplicity, self-organizing teams, regular reflection.

front: (J2) Extreme Programming (XP)
back: Agile method improving projects in five ways: communication, simplicity, feedback, respect, courage. Practices grouped into Planning, Designing, Coding (pair programming, code the unit test first, collective ownership, continuous integration) and Testing (all code has unit tests, acceptance tests).

front: (J2) Difference between a User Story and a Use Case?
back:
User Story: short sentence with the who, what and why of a requirement. Format: “As a (role) I want (something) so that (benefit)”.
Use Case: describes a behavior/interaction (cause & effect): “A user does (A), the system responds (B)”. Use cases give more detail and deeper understanding of functional behavior.

front: (J2) What is Scrum?
back:
A simple agile framework for effective team collaboration on complex software projects. It consists of Scrum Teams and their roles, events, artifacts and rules; the rules bind roles, events and artifacts together.
Highlights: Framework – Team Approach – Discipline – Collaboration – Communication.

front: (J2) What is the Scrum Team (roles)?
back:
Self-organizing & cross-functional team of three roles:
  • Product Owner – sole person responsible for managing the Product Backlog
  • Development Team – professionals delivering a potentially releasable “Done” Increment each Sprint
  • Scrum Master – promotes & supports Scrum, helps everyone understand theory, practices, rules, values

front: (J2) Scrum – Inspection & Adaptation (pillars & events)
back: Scrum is empirical, resting on transparency, inspection and adaptation. Users frequently inspect artifacts & progress toward the Sprint Goal; if something deviates outside acceptable limits, the process/material is adapted as soon as possible. Scrum prescribes four formal events for inspection & adaptation.

front: (J2) What are the four Scrum events?
back: Sprint Planning – Daily Scrum – Sprint Review – Sprint Retrospective, all happening within a Sprint (time-box ≤ 1 month). The Sprint produces a potentially releasable Increment.

front: (J2) What are the Scrum artifacts (Backlog, Sprint, Increment)?
back:
• Product Backlog: ordered list of everything known to be needed; single source of requirements (as user stories); owned by the Product Owner
  • Sprint Backlog: the set of items selected for the next iteration (Sprint)
  • Sprint: time-box of ≤ 1 month creating a “Done”, usable, potentially releasable Increment; a new Sprint starts right after the previous one
A Sprint contains Sprint Planning, Daily Scrums, the development work, Sprint Review and Sprint Retrospective.

front: (J2) What is a Scrum Taskboard?
back: A board visualizing the Sprint work, typically with columns such as To Do → In Progress → Done (stories/tasks moving across), giving the team clear visibility of progress.

front: (J2) Advantages of Scrum
back:
• Completes deliverables quickly & efficiently; effective use of time & money
  • Large projects split into manageable sprints
  • Coding & testing during the sprint review
  • Works well for fast-moving projects; clear visibility through scrum meetings
  • Adopts customer/stakeholder feedback; short sprints make changes easy
  • Individual effort visible in daily scrums

front: (J2) Disadvantages of Scrum
back:
• Often leads to scope creep (no definite end-date)
  • High failure risk if individuals aren’t committed/cooperative
  • Hard to adopt in large teams; needs experienced members
  • Daily meetings can frustrate; losing a member mid-project has big impact
  • Quality hard to ensure without aggressive testing

front: (J2) What prejudices exist about Scrum?
back: “Scrum is chaotic”, “everybody does what he wants”, “agile doesn’t fit regulated industries”, “nothing is documented”, “agile doesn’t work for distributed teams”, “Scrum solves all our problems”, “by agility we become more efficient”. → These are misconceptions; Scrum is disciplined and can be made compliant.

front: (J2) How do you make Scrum compliant with IEC 62304?
back:
Classical: map standard to processes directly. Transformation to agile: understand the standard’s goals → derive & split requirements → re-cluster them by events/artifacts → prove compliance per event/artifact → continuously adapt.
In practice: increments = sprints; continuous documentation each sprint; risk assessment during sprint planning; V&V each sprint; traceability (e.g. Jira); Definition of Done includes docs, risk analysis, V&V; plus configuration management, change control, independent reviews & training.

front: (J2) Scrum – Pig & Chicken (committed vs involved)?
back:
Pig = really committed → the Development Team members.
Chicken = only involved → Product Owner, Scrum Master, Customer, Vendor, Executives.

front: (J2) Traditional vs Agile software development?
back: Agile uses an iterative, team-based approach and requires more customer involvement. Traditional has a more formal documentation & review process. Choice depends on: speed of completion, size of the system, and level of collaboration/interaction possible among team members.

front: (J2) What did the HELENA survey show about development approaches?
back: The HELENA survey (2017/19, European perspective) found that hybrid approaches are common in practice — teams combine traditional and agile methods & practices rather than using one in pure form.

front: (J2) How do you select an appropriate development model (Ebert scheme)?
back:
Depends on complexity, project size/duration, variants, and especially requirements stability & customer involvement:
  • Requirements stable, little customer involvement → Waterfall / V-Model
  • Requirements instable, strong customer involvement → Incremental (e.g. Spiral), Prototyping / evolutionary
  • Across the board → Agile models

front: (J2) Hybrid models – the Goal × Way-to-goal matrix
back:
Model choice by how clear the Goal and the Way to the goal/solution are:
  • Goal clear + way clear → sequential (traditional)
  • increasingly unclear → incremental → iterative → extreme (agile)
Hybrid methods combine the best of classical and agile; the method must suit the company’s strategic, structural, technical and cultural situation.

front: (J2) Übung J1: Selection of Software Development Models
back: Exercise: given a project, choose a suitable model. Reasoning: hybrid methods are already often used; combine best features of classical & agile; the project-management method must be suitable for the individual situation (strategic, structural, technical and cultural components within the company).

front: How is Software Configuration Management Defined? (According to ISO/IEC/IEEE 24765:2017)
back: The application of configuration management principles to software <u>to systematically control software changes</u>, <u>maintain integrity </u>and <u>traceability</u>, and <u>ensure consistency throughout the software development lifecycle</u>.

front: What are the Key Components of Software Configuration Management?
back:
Configuration Identification:
  • Defining and managing software configuration items (CIs), such as source code, documentation, libraries, and test cases.
  • Assigning unique identifiers to track versions and dependencies.
Version Control:
  • Managing different versions of software artifacts using version control systems (VCS) like Git, SVN, or Mercurial.
Change Control:
  • Establishing processes for reviewing, approving, and implementing changes to prevent unauthorized modifications.
  • Commonly involves Change Control Boards (CCBs) in regulated industries.
Configuration Status Accounting:
  • Keeping records of configuration items, versions, changes, and updates to ensure traceability and compliance.
Configuration Auditing: 
  • Verifying that software configurations match their specifications and that all changes are properly documented.

front: Why is Software Configuration Management important?
back:
• Ensures consistency, integrity, and traceability in software development.
  • Supports regulatory compliance (e.g., ISO 13485, IEC 62304, FDA 21 CFR Part 820 for medical software).
  • Enables collaboration, rollback, and risk management in software projects.

front: What are the Regulatory Aspects of SCM (FDA 21 CFR 820, IEC 62304)?
back:
FDA 21 CFR Part 820 – §820.65 Traceability: each manufacturer shall establish procedures for identifying with a control number each unit, lot or batch of finished devices (and, where appropriate, components).

IEC 62304: as a manufacturer you must establish a scheme for the unique identification of configuration items and for controlling their versions.

front: What is the Scope of Software Configuration Management?
back:
Software systems are being changed for many reasons: Strategy and marketing decisions, bug fixing, customer requests, intrusion prevention, retired third-party components etc.

Changes have impact on many different elements:
  • Development Documents: Requirements specification, design description, Test plan, Test report, Test specification, Project plan
  • Source code files, Makefiles, resource files
  • Third-party products: Compiler, Libraries, Datenbase, ...
  • Manuals: user manual, administration manual, ...
  • Training course material, marketing brochures, ...

front: What is the Definition and Goal of Software Configuration Management?
back:
Not keeping track of modifications to one or more elements of a software system will almost certainly introduce failures and inconsistencies.

To ensure integrity and reproducibility of a software system a systematic approach is essential:
Configuration Management ... is a management process for establishing and maintaining consistency of a product’s performance, its functional and physical attributes, with its requirements, design and operational information, throughout its life. (ANSI/EIA)

front: How does the Software Configuration Process look like? (IEEE/SWEBOK)
back:
SCM controls the evolution and integrity of a product by identifying its elements; managing and controlling change; and verifying, recording, and reporting on configuration information.



Management & Planning:
  • Organizational Context: assigning roles and responsibilities, identifying interfaces and relations to other business units or projects.
  • Constraints and Guidance: considering contractual, procedural or other business restrictions, applying and promoting “Best-Practices”.
  • Planning: adapting and scaling methods and procedures to the project characteristics (size, safety, security), selecting and implementing the tools, creating an SCM Plan (SCMP).
Configuration Identification:
  • Configuration Item: an item or aggregation that is managed as a single entity.
  • Version of a Software Item: an identified instance (state) of an item.
  • Baseline: an approved and fixed (frozen) version of a configuration item at a specific time in the item’s life cycle.
  • Software Library: a controlled (identified) collection of software and related documentation designed to aid in software development, use, or maintenance.
Core Areas:
  • Control: managing changes during the software life cycle: requesting, evaluating, and approving software changes
  • Status Accounting: recording and reporting of SCM activities
  • Auditing: compliance assessment, improvement evaluation
  • Release Processing: identification, packaging, and delivery of the elements of a product (executable program, documentation, release notes, configuration data).

front: SCM Process: what belongs to Management & Planning?
back:
• Organizational Context: assigning roles & responsibilities, identifying interfaces/relations to other units or projects
  • Constraints & Guidance: considering contractual/procedural/business restrictions, applying & promoting “best practices”
  • Planning: adapting & scaling methods to project characteristics (size, safety, security), selecting/implementing tools, creating an SCM Plan (SCMP)

front: SCM Process: define the key Configuration Identification terms.
back:
• Configuration Item (CI): an item or aggregation managed as a single entity
  • Version of a software item: an identified instance (state) of an item
  • Baseline: an approved and fixed (frozen) version of a configuration item at a specific time in its life cycle
  • Software Library: a controlled (identified) collection of software and related documentation to aid development, use or maintenance

front: SCM Process: what are the Core Areas?
back:
• Control: managing changes during the life cycle — requesting, evaluating, approving changes
  • Status Accounting: recording & reporting of SCM activities
  • Auditing: compliance assessment, improvement evaluation
  • Release Processing: identification, packaging & delivery of the product elements (executable, documentation, release notes, configuration data)

front: What are Versions, Revisions and Releases?
back:
Depending on the context a version has different meanings. Multiple versions of a file (document) may exist:

They are called Revisions.

Multiple versions of a software system may exist. They are called Releases each build with a specific set of source file versions:


Revisions (versions of one file):


Releases (each a set of file versions):

front: What are Branches?
back:
Branches are used to isolate changes. Different workflow models exist to deal with branches. The main (or master) branch reflects a production-ready state. The develop branch includes the latest development changes. On the release branch only bug fixes are done.
The state of a branch can be merged into another branch.



A baseline (code or feature freeze) marks a set of changes to be included in a new release.

front: How does the Release Identification and Semantic Versioning work?
back:
Each release must have a unique identifier. Semantic versioning is a broadly accepted set of rules to build meaningful identifiers based on strictly incrementing version numbers: major.minor.patch

Major: significant (possibly incompatible) changes and extensions
Minor: added functionality
Patch: bug fixes and small improvements

front: What does Change Management include?
back:
• Managing defects, issues and change requests
  • Evaluation, prioritization, coordination
  • Review, approval and communication
  • Configuration identification
  • Merging and tracking code changes
  • Retrieval of previous versions

front: What is Software Version Control?
back:
Version control (also known as revision control, source control, or source code management) is a class of systems responsible for managing changes to computer programs, documents, web sites, or other collections of information.
Version control is a component of software configuration management.

front: What are the purposes of Software Version Control?
back:
• Version control gives access to historical versions of your project.
  • You can reproduce and understand a bug report on a past version of your software.
  • Version control enables multiple people to simultaneously work on a single project. Each person edits his or her own copy of the files and chooses when to share those changes with the rest of the team.

front: What kind of available systems for version control do exist?
back:
Centralized:
In centralized version control, each user gets his or her own working copy, but there is just one central repository. As soon as you commit, it is possible for your co-workers to update and to see your changes.

For others to see your changes, 2 things must happen: You commit, They update
Typical systems are: CVS, Subersion

Distributed
In distributed version control, each user gets his or her own repository and working copy. After you commit, others have no access to your changes until you push your changes to the central repository. A pull needs to be executed to get new data into your local repository.

For others to see your changes, 3 things must happen: You commit, You Push, They pull (fetch)
Typical systems are: Git, GNU Arch, Bazaar-NG, BitKeeper, Monotone, Mercurial

front: What is Git?
back:
Git is software for tracking changes in any set of files, usually used for coordinating work among programmers collaboratively developing source code during software development.

Its goals include speed, data integrity, and support for distributed, non-linear workflows (several branches running on different systems).

Git was created by Linus Torvalds in 2005 for development of the Linux kernel, with other kernel developers contributing to its initial development. Since 2005, Junio Hamano has been the core maintainer. As with most other distributed version control systems, and unlike most client–server systems, every Git directory on every computer is a full-fledged repository with complete history and full version-tracking abilities, independent of network access or a central server. Git is free and open-source software.

front: What are the most important features of Git?
back:
• Distributed repositories: each user gets his or her own repository and working copy.
  • Branches and tags: Branches and tags are part of the repository (compared to centralized systems)
  • Revision identifier: The SHA1 of the commit is the hash of all the information.
  • Commits are snapshots: In other version control systems, changes to individual files are tracked and referred to as revisions, but with git you are tracking the entire workspace, so they use the term snapshot to denote the difference.
  • Stages: Files have one of the following stages: untracked, modified, staged, committed

front: What is the Git Object Store?
back:
The object store contains the original data files and all the log messages, author information, dates, and other information required to rebuild any revision or branch of the project. There are 4 different object types:
  • Blob: A blob is used to store file data. It is generally a file.
  • Tree: A tree is basically like a directory. It references a bunch of other trees and blobs
  • Commit: A commit object holds metadata for each change introduced in the repository, including the author, committer, commit-data, and log messages.
  • Tag: A tag object assigns an arbitrary human-readable name to a specific object usually a commit

front: Git Object Store: what about the hash and structure?
back: Every Git object has a 160-bit SHA-1 hash (e.g. 6ff87c4664981e4397625791c8ea3bbb5f2279a3), computed from all of the object’s information — so objects are (with very high probability) globally unique. Objects are stored in the .git directory and form a directed acyclic graph (DAG).

front: What are Git Branches?
back:
One of the biggest advantages of Git compared to other systems is the easy handling of branches. Branches are needed for:
  • Executing experiments
  • Try out new ideas
  • Development of new features
In general, for any type of modification. In some software projects, it is forbidden to push directly to the master branch. Therefore it is needed to create a branch for each modification.

front: What branch types are used in a Git workflow?
back:
• Main branch
  • Development branch
  • Maintenance branch
  • Hotfix branch
  • Bugfix branch
  • Release branch
  • Feature branch
In many projects pushing directly to main/master is forbidden, so you create a branch per modification.

front: What are the important git commands?
back:
git --version
Display version of git. Also used to check whether git is installed or not.

git --config
git config - - global user . name ”David Herzig”
git config - - global user . email ”dave . herzig@gmail . com”
This information will be stored in the home directory in a file called .gitconfig
- User level (--global) - Home directory
- Repository level - Repository directory
- System level (--system) - Git installation directory

git init
Convert an existing unversioned project(workspace) to git repository or to create a new empty git repository. Executing this command creates a .git subdirectory, which contains all the metadata for the new repository. This metadata includes subdirectories for objects, refs, and template files. A HEAD file is also created which points to the current branch reference.

git clone
Download an existing git repository to your local computer. git clone -b branch_name : The -b argument lets you specify a specific branch to clone instead of the branch the remote HEAD is pointing to, usually the master branch.

git init vs git clone: At a high level, they can both be used to initialize a new git repository. However, git clone is dependent on git init. Internally, git clone first calls git init to create a new repository. It then copies the data from the existing repository.

git status
Displays:
- Current branch
- Files that have differences between Workspace - Staging area (Untracked(new) files and Unstaged changes)
- Files that have differences between Staging - Local Git Repository (Uncommitted changes)

git add 
Add changes in the workspace to the staging area. Workspace → Staging area

git commit
Add changes in the staging area to the local Git repository
git commit: Staging area → Local git repository
git commit -m "commit message"

git push
Push: Add changes in the local git repository to the remote repository
git push  <remote> <local>: Local git repository → Remote git repository

git pull
Update local git repository from the corresponding remote git repository
git pull <remote> <local>: Local git repository ← Remote git repository

git branch
List all local branches
git branch -a: List all remote branches as well
git branch -d <branch>: Delete the specified branch
git branch <new branch>: Create a new branch

git checkout <branch>
Navigate between different branches.

git merge <branch>
Integrate changes from multiple branches into one.

git remote
Manage connections to remote repositories. It allows you to show which remotes are currently connected, but also to add new connections or remove existing ones.
git remote -v: List all remote connections
git remote add: Create a new remote
connection git remote rm: Delete a connection to a remote repository
git remote rename: Rename a remote connection

git fetch <remote> <local>
Update local git repository from the corresponding remote git repository. Git fetch does not change your workspace, it keeps the fetched content separate until it is merged.

git log & git reflog
Displays information about repository changes.

.gitignore file
A text file which tells which files and folders to ignore in a project.

front: How does Git move files: Workspace → Staging → Local → Remote?
back:
Git has four areas; commands move changes between them:
  • git add: Working copy → Staging area
  • git commit: Staging area → Local repository
  • git push: Local repository → Remote repository
  • git pull / fetch: Remote → Local (fetch keeps content separate until merged)
  • git clone: copy a remote repository locally
File stages: untracked, modified, staged, committed.

front: What is a git conflict?
back: A conflict could occur if 2 or more developers work on the same file(s) or if two branches are merged.

front: How do you resolve a git conflict?
back:
A conflict can occur when 2+ developers edit the same file(s) or when two branches are merged. After a git pull you may see:
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in <file>; Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit.

The conflicting lines are marked in the file:
<<<<<<< … ======= … >>>>>>>
These must be fixed manually, then committed.

front: Git installation — how per OS?
back:
• Windows: git-scm.com/download/win
  • Ubuntu: sudo apt install git
  • macOS: git-scm.com/download/mac

front: What is the basic Git workflow without and with branches?
back:
Without branches:
git clone <url> → cd repo → (edit files) → git status → git add <file> → git commit -m "…" → git push

With branches (feature):
git branch feature/issue341 → git checkout feature/issue341 → (edit) → git add <file> → git commit -m "…" → git push --set-upstream origin feature/issue341 → git checkout main → git merge feature/issue341 → git push → (git branch -D feature/issue341)

front: Übung (Git Exercises): was sind die Schritte & was gehört (nicht) ins Repo?
back:
Steps: create a repo (GitHub/GitLab) → push your code → create a tag (e.g. V1_0_0_DATE) → verify in the web console → create a branch, make a change, merge it back into master.

Which parts belong in the repo? Source code, build/config files, documentation.
What should NOT be in the repo? Build artifacts/binaries, secrets/credentials, dependencies that can be rebuilt, large generated/IDE files — exclude them via .gitignore.

front: What is Medical Software Development and in which fields does software engineering specialize?
back:
Medical / healthcare software is a vital part of the modern healthcare industry: the process of creating and designing software applications tailored to specific healthcare use cases — built for healthcare professionals, patients, laboratories and organizations — to streamline workflows, ensure accurate data management and enhance patient care.

It is software engineering applied to a specific field/industry, e.g.: Medical, Defense, Gaming, Airspace, Business, Retail.

front: Übung: How many software components are included in a Patient Heart Rate Monitor system?
back:
Many more than you’d expect — e.g.:
  • Operating System of the smartphone
  • App on the smartphone
  • Operating System of the heart rate monitor
  • Bluetooth components
  • Centralized data collection platform
  • … and many more
Lesson: even a simple medical product involves a large, distributed set of software components.

front: What is the definition of Software Engineering?
back:
IEEE Std 610: The application of a systematic, disciplined, quantifiable approach to the development, operation and maintenance of software – i.e. the application of engineering to software.

Ian Sommerville: An engineering discipline concerned with all aspects of software production, from initial conception to operation and maintenance.

B. Boehm (1981): The application of science and mathematics by which the capabilities of computer equipment are made useful to man via programs, procedures and associated documentation.

front: What does the Standish Group report as success factors vs. factors of challenged projects?
back:
Since 1994 the Standish Group investigates software projects.

Successful projects (top factors):
  • User involvement (15.9%)
  • Executive management support (13.9%)
  • Clear statement of requirements (13.0%)
  • Proper planning (9.6%)
  • Realistic expectations (8.2%)
  • Smaller project milestones, competent staff, ownership…
Challenged projects (top factors):
  • Lack of user input (12.8%)
  • Incomplete requirements (12.3%)
  • Changing requirements (11.8%)
  • Lack of executive support (7.5%)
  • Technology incompetence (7.0%)
  • Lack of resources, unrealistic expectations…

front: What is the Standish Group conclusion („growing software“)?
back: Smaller time frames with early and frequent delivery of software components increase the success rate. Short time frames create an iterative process of design → prototype → develop → test → deploy of small elements. This is called „growing software“ (vs. „developing software“): it engages the user earlier, each component has an owner, expectations are realistic, and smaller/simpler projects reduce complexity – because complexity only causes confusion and increased cost.

front: What is the lesson of „Learning from Failures“ (Alfred Spector, 1986)?
back:
Spector compared bridge-building with software:

“Bridges are normally built on-time, on-budget, and do not fall down. Software never comes in on-time or on-budget and it always breaks down. When a bridge falls down it is investigated and a report is written on the cause of the failure. That is not the case in the computer industry, where failures are covered up, ignored and/or rationalized. As a result, we keep making the same mistakes over and over again.”

Lesson: unlike engineering, software failures are rarely analyzed – so the same mistakes are repeated.

front: Is software development a craft or engineering? (Craft & Pragmatism + Pragmatic Programmer)
back:
Jack W. Reeves (1992) asked whether software development is more a craft than an engineering discipline. Pragmatism treats words/thoughts as tools for prediction, problem-solving and action, judging ideas by their practical use and success.

Key tips from „The Pragmatic Programmer“:
  • Don’t gather requirements – dig for them; work with and think like a user
  • Make quality a requirements issue; there are no final decisions (plan for change)
  • DRY – Don’t Repeat Yourself; minimize coupling; don’t live with „broken windows“
  • Always use source code control; refactor early & often (Boy-Scout principle)
  • Design to test; test early, often, automatically; sign your work

front: How has Software Maintenance Cost developed over time?
back:
Studies (Boehm ‘80, Lientz ‘87, US DoD ‘97) show the cost share shifting massively from hardware to software maintenance:
  • 1955: ~85% hardware, little software
  • 1985: maintenance ~50%
  • 2000: maintenance ~70%, development ~20%, hardware ~10%
Operation and maintenance costs can exceed development costs by factors.

front: Beispiel (failure): Was war der Ariane 5 Software-Fehler?
back: Classic software-failure example shown in the lecture (alongside Therac-25). On its maiden flight (1996) the Ariane 5 rocket self-destructed shortly after launch: reused Ariane-4 software tried to convert a 64-bit floating-point number into a 16-bit integer → numeric overflow / unhandled exception in the inertial reference system. Lesson: reused software must be re-validated for its new context.

front: Übung (Exercises): die 3 Fragen aus der Vorlesung (slides_01)
back:
1. What’s important when checking a team member’s code? Code review — check correctness, readability, coding standards/conventions, tests and security; give constructive feedback.
  2. How would you assure software quality? Reviews, unit & automated testing, static code analysis, continuous integration, coding standards, adherence to IEC 62304.
  3. How would you handle a project that’s behind schedule? Re-prioritize scope (MVP), communicate early with stakeholders, cut non-essential features, re-plan realistically, add focused help — avoid simply adding people late.

back: Medical device software

front: What is the name of the european/swiss counterpart of the american FDA
back:
