  • Swissmedic (Swiss)

front: What is the mission of the FDA?
back:
• Efficacy
  • Safety - Usability
  • Security - Cybersecurity

"The FDA is responsible for protecting the public health by ensuring the safety, efficacy, and security of human and veterinary drugs, biological products, and medical devices; and by ensuring the safety of our nation’s food supply, cosmetics, and products that emit radiation"

front: What software is not subject to FDA regulations?
back:
• Software for administrative support of health care facility
  • Software to serve as electronic patient records
  • Software for maintaining or encouraging a healthy lifestyle
  • Software for transferring, storing, converting or displaying data
  • Software to provide certain, limited clinical decision support

front: What is the history of the FDA and its key milestones?
back:
Laws are generally reactive — each disaster triggered new regulation:
  • 1906 Food & Drug Act – against poisonous food additives
  • 1938 Food, Drug & Cosmetics Act – after Elixir Sulfanilamide (antibiotic) killed 100+ people; expanded FDA scope to cosmetics & therapeutic devices, authorized factory inspections
  • 1962 Thalidomide disaster (~10,000 children born with disabilities) → Congress requires FDA approval before market release
  • 1971 Dalkon Shield (IUD; ~2.5M women; pelvic inflammatory disease, infertility, even death) → Medical Device Amendment requires premarket approval for medical devices

front: Why does the FDA not allow companies to reschedule audits?
back: Because audits are meant to ensure compliance. Giving a company more time would let it tidy up irregularities before the inspection. (FDA factory inspections are authorized with or without registration, i.e. can be unannounced.)

front: What was the Therac-25 incident and its lessons?
back:
The Therac-25 (1982) was a radiation-therapy machine with three modes (Electron, X-Ray, Field-Light); the X-Ray mode used ~100× more energy with a flattener, and software ensured safety. Between 1985–1987 there were 6 accidents with massive radiation overdoses.

Investigation outcome:
  • Requirements & design documentation written as an afterthought
  • No quality systems during design/development
  • Confusing UI, cryptic error messages (“Malfunction 54”)
  • Overly complicated design; only system-level testing, no unit testing
Key lesson: Safety is a quality of the system in which the software is used — not a quality of the software itself. (Note: X-rays have deeper penetration than electrons.)

front: What does regulation of medical software mean?
back: Regulation refers to the rules, laws and standards that govern the development, use and distribution of software used in healthcare. They ensure medical software is safe, effective and compliant with public-health standards, protecting patients, providers and organizations from risks like data breaches, inaccurate diagnoses and ineffective treatments.

front: What is the definition of a medical device (WHO)?
back: Medical devices include all health technologies (except vaccines and medicines) required for prevention, diagnosis, treatment, monitoring, rehabilitation and palliation. They are indispensable for universal health coverage, monitoring wellbeing and addressing outbreaks or emergencies.

front: What are examples of medical devices (WHO)?
back:
• Single use devices (syringes, catheters)
  • Implantable (hip prothesis, pacemakers)
  • Imaging (ultrasound, CT-scanners)
  • Medical equipment (anesthesia machines, patient monitors)
  • Software (computer aided diagnostics)
  • In vitro diagnostics (glucometer, HIV tests)
  • Personal protective equipment (mask, gowns, gloves)
  • Surgical and labratory instruments

front: What is the Definition of Software as Medical Device (FDA)?
back: Software intended to be used for one or more medical purposes that perform these purposes without being part of a hardware medical device

front: How do you determine if your product is a medical device (SaMD)?
back:
Two questions:
  1. Is the product a medical device?
  2. Is the software function intended for a medical purpose?
If yes, it must be treated as Software as a Medical Device (SaMD). Note: software not classified as a medical device today may become one tomorrow.

front: What are examples of SaMD (Software as a medical device) and what is not?
back:
• Diagnosis, prevention, monitoring, treatment or alleviation of disease
  • Disease management
  • Control of conception
  • In-vitro diagnostics and sterillization
Not SaMD examples:
  • A software that is embedded as part of a hardware medical device and is necessary to drive the intended medical purpose
  • Software to serve as electronic patient records
  • Software for administrative support of heatlh care facility
  • Software to maintain a healthy lifestyle

front: What is the difference between Laws, Regulations and Guidance?
back:
• Laws: Laws are passed by both houses of the U.S. Congress and then signed by the president
  • Regulations: Agencies are tasked with implementing laws by drafting regulation. They take the broad ideas of the bill and fill in the details of what needs to be done and how it will be enforced.
  • Guidance: Guidance is supplemental material published by an agency that helps clarify existing rules. These include interagency statements, advisories, bulletins, policy statements, questions and answers and frequently asked questions. It is not subject to rule-making procedures, so there is no proposal or comment period. Guidance can be helpful, but it is not binding.

front: What are concrete examples of Regulations vs. Guidance (US & EU)?
back:
• QSR – Quality System Regulation (US regulation)
  • GPSV – General Principles of Software Validation (US guidance)
  • MDR – Medical Device Regulation (EU regulation)
  • MDCG – Guidance on Cybersecurity for medical devices (EU guidance)

front: What are Industry Standards for Medical Software Development?
back:
• ISO 13485: Medical Devices - Quality Management Systems
  • ISO 14971: Application of Risk Management to medical devices
  • IEC 62304: Medical device software - systems lifecycle process
  • IEC 62366: Medical devices - Application of usability engineering to medical devices
  • IEC 82304: Health Software
  • IEC 60601: Medical electrical equipment
A set of criteria within an industry relating to the standard functioning and carrying out of operations in their respective fields of production. In other words, it is the generally accepted requirements followed by the members of an industry

front: What is important in terms of software classification?
back: If a software product is classified as medical device, software engineers must pay attention to different regulations. The decision on whether a software should be classified as a medical device is up to the manufacturer themselves.

front: How is SaMD risk-classified (IMDRF / MDR Rule 11)?
back:
The IMDRF (International Medical Device Regulators Forum) gives a framework for risk categorization of SaMD, based on (a) the significance of the information for the healthcare decision and (b) the state of the healthcare situation (critical / serious / non-serious) → categories I–IV (IV = highest risk).
In the EU, MDR Classification Rule 11 governs medical-device software. This guidance is not legally binding; the classification decision is up to the manufacturer.

front:
A digital biomarker application (collecting data from a smart device) is used to drive clinical management decisions for patient with serious healthcare conditions. What is the correct medical device classification for that software?

What could be an additional benefit for the company to achieve SaMD regulatory approval?
back:
ChatGPT:

Under the widely used IMDRF risk framework, a digital biomarker application that “drives clinical management” for a “serious” condition typically falls into SaMD Category III

An additional benefit of obtaining SaMD regulatory approval (beyond mere compliance) is that it can open doors to reimbursement, expand market access, and build clinical and commercial credibility for the product.

front: How does the Regulatory Approval process work?
back:
1. Establish quality process for software creation
  2. Design software and document
  3. Perform risk analysis
  4. Review process with FDA (optional)
  5. Implement software and document
  6. Perform validation study
  7. Submit entire process to FDA
  8. Approval/Clearance
  9. Post-market monitoring
To get approval/clearance for a SaMD product, the regulation body (e.g. FDA) will not test your code/software. The regulation body will review/check if there is a process in place and there is a proof that the process was followed correctly.

front: Regulatory approval — how does it NOT work?
back:
It does not work like this: 1) implement software → 2) perform validation study → 3) submit results → 4) approval.

Instead the FDA reviews the entire process: establish a quality process, design & document, risk analysis, implement & document, validate, then submit the whole process. The FDA does not test your code — it checks that a process exists and was followed correctly.

front: What does the Quality System Regulation do?
back:
The quality system regulation includes requirements related to the methods used in, and the facilities and controls used for, designing, manufacturing, packaging, labeling, storing, installing, and servicing of medical devices intended for human use.

Consists of 10 parts:
A: General Provisions
B: Quality System Requirements
C: Design Controls
D: Document Controls
E: Purchasing Controls
F: ...

B: Quality System Requirements:
  • Management Responsibility
  • Quality Audit
  • Personnel
C: Design Controls: Each manufacturer of any class III or class II device, and the class I devices listed in paragraph ... of this section, shall establish and maintain procedures to control the design of the device in order to ensure that specified design requirements are met.

front: What is the difference between Verification and Validation?
back:
Verification: Specified requirements have been fulfilled
Validation: Particular requirements for a specific intended use can be conistently fulfilled

front: What does <u>Design input</u>, <u>Specification</u>, <u>Design Output</u>, <u>Design Review </u>and <u>Design history file </u>mean?
back:
Design input:  Means the physical and performance requirements of a device that are used as a basis for device design. IEC 62304: The interpretation of customer needs into formally documented medical device requirements.

Specification: Means any requirement with which a product, process service, or other activity must conform. IEC 62304: ...are the formally documented specifications of what the software does to meet the customer needs and the design inputs.

Design Output: Means the result of a design effort at each design phase and at the end of the total design effort.

Design reviews: Means a documented, comprehensive, systematic examination of a design to evaluate the adequacy of the design requirements, to evaluate the capability of the design to meet these requirements, and to identify problems.

Design history file: Means a compilation of records which describes the design history of a finished device.

front: Why are formal documents required in the design process (QSR)?
back: In a formal design process you need formal documents — signed, dated and properly archived — that verify the process was followed. The regulator checks the documented process, not the code itself.

front: What is Software Validation (in detail)?
back:
FDA:  Validation is the confirmation by objective evidence, that the previously established requirements for the use of a process or system are met.

This is widely used in the Pharmaceutical, Life Sciences and BioTech industries and is a cousin of Software Testing but with a more formal and documented approach. The validation process begins with validation planning, system requirements definition, testing and verification activities, and validation reporting. The system lifecycle then enters the operational phase and continues until system retirement and retention of system data based on regulatory rules.

front: How should software be devloped?
back:
This guidance recommends an integration of software life cycle management and risk management activities. Based on the intended use and the safety risk associated with the software to be developed, the software developer should determine the specific approach, the combination of techniques to be used, and the level of effort to be applied.

Example: The V-Model

front: Examples and uses of Software Life Cycle models
What needs to be done for Software Validation?
back:
• Software Requirements Specification
  • Implementations and Traceability
  • Software Architecture
  • Third-Party Libraries (SOUP - Software of Unknown Proveance)
  • Verification

front: What is the structure of the GPSV (General Principles of Software Validation)?
back:
FDA guidance applicable to validating medical-device software (and software used to design/develop/manufacture devices). Six parts:
  1. Purpose
  2. Scope
  3. Context for Software Validation
  4. Principles of Software Validation
  5. Activities and Tasks
  6. Validation of Automated Process Equipment

front: Which life-cycle model must you use for validated software?
back: The GPSV does not prescribe a specific model — you may choose whatever model you want (often the V-Model), as long as you document it and adhere to your own development process, which itself must comply with IEC 62304. Verification & validation must run throughout the entire life cycle.

front: What do the FDA recall statistics say about software changes?
back: FDA analysis of 3140 medical-device recalls (1992–1998): 242 (7.7%) were attributable to software failures. Of those, 192 (79%) were caused by defects introduced when changes were made to the software after its initial production/distribution. → Changes are the main source of software defects.

front: Why is software verification & validation difficult?
back: Because a developer cannot test forever and it is hard to know how much evidence is enough. You must build a level of confidence that the device meets all requirements and user expectations. The necessary level of confidence depends on the safety risk imposed by the device’s automated functions.

front: What are the key differences between hardware and software (for validation)?
back:
• Most software problems stem from errors in design & development
  • Testing alone usually can’t verify software is complete/correct → combine with other verification + a documented process
  • Software is not physical and does not wear out
  • Updates can introduce defects
  • Software failures occur without advance warning
  • Seemingly insignificant code changes can cause big problems elsewhere
  • Maintainers may not be the original developers → accurate documentation is essential
→ Software engineering needs even greater scrutiny/control than hardware engineering.

front: Is software testing alone sufficient for validation?
back: No. Software testing is necessary but in most cases not sufficient to establish confidence that the software is fit for its intended use. Developers should use a mixture of methods and techniques to both prevent errors and detect errors that do occur.

front: What is a core principle of software validation (about specification)?
back: You need a formal specification: software validation cannot be completed without an established software/system requirements specification. (Common startup mistake: develop first and “worry about the FDA later” when validating — this does not work.)

front: What are the activities and tasks of software validation?
back:
• Quality planning
  • Requirements
  • Designs
  • Construction / implementation / coding
  • Testing by the developer
  • User-site testing
  • Maintenance and software changes

front: How are external / off-the-shelf components handled in validation?
back: When software (e.g. libraries) is developed by someone other than the device manufacturer, that developer may not be directly responsible for FDA compliance. The party with regulatory responsibility (the device manufacturer) must assess the adequacy of the off-the-shelf developer’s activities and determine what additional efforts are needed to establish the software is validated for the intended use. (Related: SOUP – Software of Unknown Provenance.)

front: Question: What general (software development) techniques could be used to improve defect prevention?
back:
• Using code reviews
  • Automated testing
  • Static code analysis
  • Adherence to ISO/IEC 62304 helps prevent defects early in development.

front: Question: What techniques could be used to prevent defects after changes?
back: Regression testing, automated continuous integration, version control, and thorough impact analysis ensure that new changes don’t introduce defects.

front: Question: What is the difference between validation and verification?
back: Verification checks if the software meets specifications, while validation ensures the software fulfills its intended purpose in real-world conditions.

front: Question: Which software life cycle models are good fits for validated software?
back: The V-Model and Waterfall Model are commonly used for validated software due to their structured approach and traceability.

front: Question: What techniques could be used to trace a bug/issue?
back:
• Logging
  • Debugging tools
  • Issue tracking systems (e.g., Jira)
  • Root cause analysis

front: Difference between "Software in a Medical Device" and "Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)"?
back:
• Software in a Medical Device: Software that is embedded in and necessary to operate a hardware medical device (e.g., software controlling an x-ray machine).
  • Software as a Medical Device (SaMD): Software that performs a medical function without being part of a hardware device (e.g., diagnosis software for breast cancer)

front: When does software qualify as a medical device?
back: Software qualifies as a medical device when it is intended for medical purposes such as diagnosis, treatment, prevention, or monitoring of diseases, regardless of whether it’s embedded in hardware or standalone

front: Are FDA guidance documents legally binding?
back: No, guidance documents are not legally binding. They help clarify existing rules but are not subject to rule-making procedures

front: What is the FDA looking for during the SaMD approval process? Is it the final product?
back:
The FDA does not evaluate the final product alone. Instead, it assesses whether:
  • A formal quality process was followed
  • Documentation of design, risk analysis, validation, etc. exists
  • The entire process complies with regulatory expectations
So, it’s the process, not just the final software, that matters.

front: What fraction of software-related recalls were due to software updates (not original version)?
back:
According to the FDA's analysis:
  • 7.7% of 3140 medical device recalls were due to software failures
  • Of these, 79% were caused by software defects introduced during updates after initial release

front: SaMD that analyzes images, eye movement, etc. to guide astigmatism diagnosis – what classification is that?
back:
This would likely fall under IMDRF Class III or IV, depending on:
  • The severity of the condition being diagnosed
  • Whether it drives clinical decisions
Given it influences next diagnostic actions for a vision disorder, it's a moderate to high-risk SaMD.

front: According to IEC 62304, what should be part of your software lifecycle process?
back:
IEC 62304 outlines a comprehensive software lifecycle process for medical device software. Key required activities include:
  • Software Development Planning
  • Software Requirements Analysis
  • Software Architecture and Design
  • Software Implementation and Unit Testing
  • Software Integration and Integration Testing
  • System Testing
  • Software Maintenance
  • Risk Management throughout the lifecycle
  • Configuration and Problem Resolution Management
This ensures traceability, quality, and safety across the software's entire lifecycle

front: Does the FDA prescribe a particular software life cycle model?
back:
No, the FDA does not prescribe a specific software life cycle model. Instead, it requires that:
  • A formal development process is followed
  • The process must be documented
  • The selected model must comply with IEC 62304 principles
You can use models like Waterfall, V-Model, Agile, etc., as long as they are properly documented and adhered to

front: At what point in the software life cycle does one perform risk management?
back:
Risk management must be integrated throughout the entire software life cycle. It begins early, during:
  • Requirements and design phases (to identify and mitigate hazards)
  • And continues through
    - Implementation
    - Testing
    - Maintenance and updates
This aligns with both IEC 62304 and FDA expectations.

front: Are agile processes compatible with FDA regulations?
back:
Yes, agile processes are compatible with FDA regulations if:
  • The development process is well-documented
  • All regulatory requirements (e.g., validation, verification, risk management) are met
  • There is traceability from user needs to final implementation
  • Agile artifacts (e.g., user stories, sprints) are supplemented with the necessary documentation
FDA is flexible in how you develop software, but strict on demonstrating compliance and quality

front: What is the primary purpose of configuration management in software development?
back:
The primary purpose is to systematically control software changes, ensuring:
  • Consistency, integrity, and traceability throughout the software lifecycle
  • That all components are identified, tracked, and auditable
  • Compliance with regulations like IEC 62304, ISO 13485, and FDA 21 CFR Part 820

front: What is the difference between a centralized and distributed version control system?
back:
Centralized Version Control System (CVCS):
  • One central repository
  • Developers commit directly to the central server
  • Examples: CVS, Subversion (SVN)
  • Workflow: commit > update
Distributed Version Control Systems (DVCS):
  • Each developer has a local repository and working copy
  • Changes are comitted locally first, then pushed
  • Others must pull to receive changes
  • Examples: Git, Mercurial
  • Workflow: commit > push > pull

front: What does git commit do?
back:
• Moves changes from the staging area into the local repository
  • Saves a snapshot of the current project state

front: What is the purpose of branching?
back:
• Isolate changes for experiments, features, bug fixes or releases
  • Enable parallel development without affecting the main codebase
  • Protect the master/main banch from unstable code
  • Allow merging once changes are tested and reviewed

front: What does the basic COCOMO model primarily use to estimate the effort required for a software project?
back: The basic COCOMO model (Constructive Cost Model by Barry Boehm) estimates software project effort using the formula:

front: What does the critical path in a project network represent?
back:
The critical path is the longest path through the project network. It determines the shortest possible project duration. Tasks on this path:
  • Cannot be delayed without delaying the entire project
  • Represent the most time-sensitive activities in the plan

front: If a task is not on the critical path, what does that imply?
back:
If a task is not on the critical path, it implies that:
  • The task has slack or float time
  • Its start or end can be delayed without affecting the total project duration
These tasks offer scheduling flexibility, allowing better resource distribution

front: What are typical ways on how to describe requirements?
back:
• User Stories (Agile approach)
  • Use Cases (Unified Process)
  • UML Diagrams (Class, Sequence, State-Transitions)
  • Mockups (UI Design)
  • Data Flow Diagrams, Entity-Relationship Diagrams
  • Structured documents like Software Requirements Specifications (SRS)

front: What is the difference between functional and non-functional requirements?
back:
Functional Requirements: Describe what the system should do.
Examples: input/output behavior, actions, triggers, use cases

Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs): Describe how the system performs or the constraints.
Examples: performance, security, maintainability, usability, availability

front: Why is it important when meeting with users to try to restate the problem in your own words?
back:
Restating the problem in your own words helps:
  • Ensure you understand the user's actual needs
  • Catch hidden assumptions or unclear requirements
  • Build trust and alignment between stakeholders and developers

front: What is the role of the revision control page?
back:
The revision control page in a design document:
  • Tracks versions, changes, and updates
  • Ensures traceability and accountability
  • Helps teams maintain a stable reference document throughout development

front:
Is this a functional or a non-functional requirement?

"The system should export the result in a PowerPoint file which can be shared with the referring physician."
back: This is a functional requirement, because it specifies a specific action or behavior of the system (i.e., exporting results in a certain format)

front: What should a software designer do if he/she identifies a problem with the system’s requirements during the creation of the software design document?
back:
They should:
  • Report the issue immediately
  • Communicate with stakeholders or requirements engineers
  • Clarify or request updates to the SRS document
  • Avoid proceeding with design based on unclear or flawed requirements

front: What is the purpose of a UML class diagram, and what key elements does it include?
back:
Shows the static structure of a system—classes and relationships.

Key elements:
  • Class: name, attributes, methods
  • Visibility: + public, − private, # protected
  • Relationships: association, inheritance, aggregation, composition

front: Draw and describe a use case diagram for a simple online bookstore. Include actors and at least three use cases.
Explain the singleton pattern.
back: Ensures only one instance of a class exists.

front: What does CRISP means in a build process?
back:
Complete
Repeatable
Informative
Schedulable
Portable

front: What are typical systems used for the build process?
back:
• Make, CMake
  • Apahe Ant
  • Apache Maven
  • Gradle
  • CI systems: Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD

front: What are typical tasks fulfilled in a build process?
back:
• Fetch code from version control
  • Compile code
  • Run unit tests
  • Link libraries
  • Generate artifacts (e.g. .jar, .exe)
  • Create documentation
  • Archvie logs

front: How does dependency management work in a modern build system?
back:
Modern tools (like Maven, Gradle, pip) manage dependencies by:
  • Declaring them in config files (pom.xml, build.gradle, requirements.txt)
  • Automatically downloading required versions
  • Handling version conflicts and scopes (test, runtime, etc.)

front: What is a coverage report?
back: A coverage report shows how much of the source code is tested by automated tests (e.g., unit tests), identifying tested vs untested code

front: Why is a high coverage grade good?
back:
• Ensure code is thoroughly tested
  • Detect dead/unreachable code
  • Reduce bugs and improve reliability

front: Headless vs Headful Web Testing
back:
• Headful: UI is rendered
  • Headless: No GUI, runs faster and ideal for CI/CD pipelines

front: What is remote debugging?
back: Debugging a program running on a different machine than the one running the debugger

front: What is an assertion?
back:
• A condition in code that must always be true
  • Used to detect bugs during development and testing

front: Why perform memory analysis?
back: To detect memory leaks or inefficient usage, which can crash the system over time

front: Advantages of a logging system:
back:
• Easier debugging
  • Error tracking
  • System monitoring
  • Audit trail creation

front: Typical logging appenders
back:
• Console
  • File
  • E-Mail (SMTP)
  • Database (JDBC)
  • Syslog
  • Socket

front: What is Continuous Integration (CI)?
back: Practice of frequent code integration into a shared repo, with automated builds and tests to catch issues early

front: How to define a CI pipeline in GitLab?
back: By adding a .gitlab-ci.yml file that defines stages (build, test, deploy) and scripts for each job.

front: What should be used as input for unit tests?
back: Isolated units like functions or methods, using mocked data or parameters

front: What should be used as input for system tests?
back: Entire system with real or simulated external components (DB, APIs, UI) using realistic user scenarios

front: What are typical issues when it comes to documentation?
back:
• No documentation created at all
  • Outdated documentation that quickly becomes irrelelvant
  • Unnecessary documentation that nobody reads or uses
  • Ignored documentation, even if it exists and is usful
These problems reduce communication efficiency, increase onboarding time, and raise maintenance difficulty.

front: What is the CIA triangle?
back:
• Confidentiality - Prevent unauthorized access to data
  • Integrity - Ensure data is not altered or tampered with
  • Availability - Ensure systems and data are accessible when neeeded

front: Difference between Authentication and Authorization
back:
• Authentication: Confirms who you are (e.g., login with password)
  • Authorization: Defines what you’re allowed to do (e.g., access admin panel)

front: What types of attacks do you know?
back:
• Injection attacks (e.g. SQL injection)
  • Cross-site scripting (XSS)
  • Session hijacking
  • Denial of Service (DOS)
  • Brute-force attacks

front: Difference between Symmetric and Asymmetric Encryption
back: Symmetric encryption uses the same key for both encryption and decryption. In contrast, asymmetric encryption uses a pair of keys—a public key to encrypt and a private key to decrypt.

front: What does Analytical Validation mean?
back: It verifies that the software correctly processes input data to produce accurate, reliable, and precise outputs as intended (e.g., algorithm correctness, data handling)

front: What does Clinical Validation mean?
back: It confirms that the software achieves its intended medical purpose in the target user population within a real clinical context

front: When is an IDE required to test a medical device?
back: An Investigational Device Exemption (IDE) is required when testing a significant-risk medical device on humans, to allow its use in clinical trials before full regulatory approval

front: What does decommissioning mean?
back: Decommissioning (or software retirement) is the planned discontinuation of a software product, including stopping support, migrating data, and removing it from operation

front: What are typical mechanisms to deploy a piece of software?
back:
• Mobile app stores
  • Rich client installers
  • Web applications
  • Docker containers
  • Package managers (pip, npm)

front: Why is maintenance important (even if there is no change in the software itself)?
back: Because of evolving regulations, security vulnerabilities, infrastructure changes, and user environment updates, maintenance ensures the system stays compliant, secure, and functional

front: Please explain the Waterfall model (what, when to use it, advantages/disadvantages)
back:
What:
A linear, step-by-step software development model with phases like:
Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance.

When:
  • Requirements are clear and fixed
  • High need for documentation or regulatory compliance
  • Projects are small or simple
Pros:
  • Easy to understand and manage
  • Clear structure and milestones
  • Good for well-defined projects
Cons:
  • Inflexible to changes
  • Testing happens late
  • Poor fit for complex or evolving projects

front: Please explain the V-Model model (what, when to use it, advantages/disadvantages)
back:
What:
An extension of the Waterfall model where each development phase has a corresponding testing phase, forming a "V" shape. Emphasizes validation at every stage.

When:
  • Projects with clear, stable requirements
  • Safety-critical systems (e.g. medical, aerospace)
  • Need for early and rigorous testing

Pros:
  • Early error detection
  • Strong quality assurance
  • Clear links between dev & test phases

Cons:
  • Rigid and costly to change
  • Not suited for agile/uncertain projects

front: Please explain the SCRUM model (what, when to use it, advantages/disadvantages)
back:
What:
An iterative, team-based Agile framework for developing complex products. Work is done in fixed-length cycles called Sprints (usually 1–4 weeks).

Key Roles:
  • Product Owner – defines and prioritizes features (Product Backlog)
  • Scrum Master – facilitates the process, removes blockers
  • Development Team – builds the product
When:
  • Projects with changing or unclear requirements
  • Teams that value collaboration and flexibility
  • Fast-paced environments needing frequent delivery
Pros:
  • Adaptable to change
  • Encourages team communication
  • Frequent feedback improves product quality
Cons:
  • Needs experienced, disciplined teams
  • Less emphasis on documentation
  • Can be hard to scale for large teams

front: Please explain the Unified Process model (what, when to use it, advantages/disadvantages)
back:
What:
An iterative and incremental software development framework that focuses on architecture, risk management, and use cases.

Phases:
  1. Inception – define scope and business case
  2. Elaboration – design architecture, address high risks
  3. Construction – build the product incrementally
  4. Transition – deliver to users, refine based on feedback
When:
  • Large, complex, or high-risk systems
  • Teams using object-oriented design (e.g., UML)
  • Projects needing formal processes with flexibility
Pros:
  • Risk-focused and architecture-driven
  • Scalable and customizable
  • Supports iterative development
Cons:
  • Can be complex and heavyweight
  • May be overkill for small projects
  • Needs training and tooling

front: What is Configuration Management?
back:
Configuration Management is a structured process used to:
  • Identify, organize, and control changes to software artifacts (like code, documentation, requirements)
  • Ensure consistency and traceability throughout the software lifecycle
  • Keep track of different versions, who changed what, when, and why
It supports:
  • Version Control
  • Change tracking
  • Release management
  • Auditability
In regulated environments (like medical software), configuration management is mandatory to ensure compliance with standards like IEC 62304, FDA 21 CFR 820, and ISO 13485.

front: What are tags in Git?
back:
• A named snapshot of the code at a specific point
  • Often used to mark releases (e.g., v1.0.0)
  • Tags are read-only references

front: What are important Git Operations?
back:
• git clone: copy a remote repo
  • git add: stage changes
  • git commit: save changes locally
  • git push: upload to remote
  • git pull: fetch updates from remote
  • git merge: combine branches
  • git checkout: switch versions or branches
  • git status: see current changes

front: Why are Branches imprtant?
back: Used to isolate development (e.g., features, bugfixes)

front: What does the SRS bridge?
back: Bridge between user needs and software design

front: What does the Technical System Design (TSD) bridge?
back: Bridge between user needs and the process of coding

front: What are Design Patterns?
back: Reusable solution for a common problem (e.g. singleton, factory)

front: What kinds of UML do exist?
back:
• Structure Diagram (e.g. Class diagram)
  • Behavior Diagram (e.g. Use Case diagram)

front: What are tools for Source Code Analysis?
back:
• Sonar
  • PyLint

front:
What do the following terms mean?

  • Debug
  • Assertions
  • Unit Testing
  • Web Testing
  • Load Tests
  • Logging
  • Continous integration
back:
• Debug: Debugging is the process of identifying and fixing errors or bugs in the software by analyzing code behavior and execution. 

  • Assertions: Assertions are conditions placed in the code to automatically check that certain assumptions hold true during execution.
     
  • Unit Testing: Unit testing involves testing individual components or functions of a program to ensure they work as expected in isolation.

  • Web Testing: Web testing checks a web application for functionality, usability, compatibility, performance, and security.

  • Load Tests: Load testing evaluates how a system performs under expected or heavy user loads to identify performance bottlenecks.
     
  • Logging: Logging records runtime events and messages from a program to help with monitoring, debugging, and audits.

  • Continous integration: Continuous integration is the automated process of building and testing code every time changes are committed to a shared repository.

front: Difference between Verification and Validation
back:
Verification: Specified requirements have been fullfilled
Validation: Particular requirements for a specific intended use can be consistently fulfilled.

front: What is the Why / What / How structure of Requirements Engineering?
back:
RE moves top-down from purpose to solution:
  • Why? → Define Goals: Vision, Stakeholder, Frame Conditions, Goals & Risks
  • What? → Understand Context: Business Processes, System Context
  • What? → Gather Requirements: Use Cases & User Stories, Quality Requirements
  • How? → Design Solution: Data model, Templates, Reports, Interfaces, Technical Conception

front: Which questions guide each Requirements-Engineering level?
back:
• Vision: use & scope on a higher level?
  • Stakeholder: who benefits / influences / is affected?
  • Frame Conditions: technical conditions, timeframe, budget?
  • Goals & Risk: goals, benefit, where are risks?
  • Business Processes: how does process integration look?
  • System Context: need for system integration?
  • Use Cases & User Stories: which functions are required?
  • Quality Requirements: which quality status, regulation dependencies?
  • Data model/templates/reports/interfaces: what is needed to realize the system?
  • Technical conception: how will functionality be built?

front: What are the key principles of Requirements Engineering?
back:
• Define/refine requirements step-by-step — from a general (“global”) perspective to a detailed view
  • Think about testing for each and every requirement
  • Complex software-development projects necessarily lead to complex test requirements

front: What are the characteristics of a good SRS, and what are C- vs D-Requirements?
back:
A good Software Requirements Specification (SRS) is: Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked for importance/stability, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable.

SRS per IEEE Std 830-1998 as a joint approach:
  • C-Requirement (customer requirements) → „Lastenheft“
  • D-Requirement (developer requirements) → „Pflichtenheft“

front: What is the content & structure of an SRS (IEEE 830)?
back:
1. Introduction (1.1 Purpose, 1.2 Scope, 1.3 Definitions/acronyms, 1.4 References, 1.5 Overview)
2. Overall description (2.1 Product perspective, 2.2 Product functions, 2.3 User characteristics, 2.4 Constraints, 2.5 Assumptions & dependencies)
3. Specific requirements + Appendixes + Index.
